# Spatial foundation models for the Bioconductor community {#sec-foundation-models}

Source code: <https://github.com/drighelli/BiocFoundationGPU>

## Motivation

Foundation models for spatial data are predominantly distributed as Python
packages, and configuring them for a particular compute environment is often
fragile: most have specific version dependencies that are not always
documented, even when the model is on PyPI. This makes individual models
hard to install for many R users and makes reproducible benchmarking across
models onerous. The `BiocFoundationGPU` package addresses both problems.

## Package overview

`BiocFoundationGPU` configures and runs foundation-model pipelines from R.
It uses `basilisk` and `reticulate` to build separate environments per model
(@fig-bfg-schema), avoiding the dependency conflicts that arise when
attempting to satisfy all models in a single environment. The package ships a
collection of `BasiliskEnvironment` schemas that pin a Python version and the
required PyPI dependencies for each model. For models not on PyPI,
`BiocFoundationGPU` provides template scripts to build environments via
`reticulate`.

![Schematic of the `BiocFoundationGPU` package. Input data in
`SpatialExperiment` is fed to functions that set up isolated Python
environments via `reticulate`, run the model, and return embeddings into
the `SpatialExperiment` object.](../figures/team_foundationmodels/schema.png){#fig-bfg-schema width=85%}

For each supported model, the package exports a `Run_<model>()` function
(e.g., `Run_novae()`). The package focuses on **zero-shot** use of
pre-trained models: input data are passed in and embeddings come back, with
no fine-tuning required. The final design takes a `SpatialExperiment` as
input and writes embeddings to its `reducedDims` slot.

## Models

Six models spanning the breadth of current spatial-foundation use cases were
prioritized (@tbl-bfg-models). Wherever possible, weights are pulled from
Hugging Face so the entire workflow runs through the package; the
exception is scGPT, which uses upstream PyTorch checkpoints.

| Status | Model | Category | PyPI | Primary input | Output | Embedding size |
| :----: | :---- | :------- | :--: | :------------ | :----- | :------------- |
| ✅ | scGPT [@cui2024scgpt] | Single-cell transcriptomics | ✅ | Gene counts | Cell embeddings | 512 |
| 🟡 | scFoundation [@hao2024scfoundation] | Single-cell transcriptomics | ✅ | Gene counts | Gene / cell embeddings | 3072 |
| ✅ | Novae [@blampey2024novae] | Spatial transcriptomics | ✅ | Gene counts + spatial coords | Neighbor-context embedding + zero-shot domains | 64 |
| 🟡 | Prov-GigaPath [@xu2024gigapath] | H&E imaging | ❌ | H&E WSI tiles | Tile / slide embeddings | tile 1536, slide 768 |
| ✅ | Nimbus [@rumberger2025automated] | Spatial proteomics | ✅ | Multiplex TIFF + segmentation mask | Per-cell expression estimates and annotations | — |
| ❌ | KRONOS [@shaban2025foundation] | Spatial proteomics | ❌ | TIFF + mask + marker annotation | Per-patch embeddings | 384 |

: Models packaged in `BiocFoundationGPU`. ✅ = exposed via `Run_*()`,
🟡 = environment recipe shipped but no wrapper yet, ❌ = not currently
runnable through the package. {#tbl-bfg-models}

### Model implementations

**scGPT** [@cui2024scgpt] is a foundation model for single-cell
transcriptomics. While not designed for spatial data, transcriptional
profiles from spatial assays can be passed through it for comparison with
spatial-specific models. (A spatial extension of scGPT is also available
[@wang2025scgptspatial] but is not yet integrated.) Our implementation
uses upstream PyTorch checkpoints (`.pt` plus support `.json` files) rather
than the Hugging Face port, because scGPT distributes multiple pretrained
checkpoints and working with them directly preserves user choice. `Run_scGPT()`
takes the path to an `.h5ad` file and the directory containing the
pretrained model.

**Novae** [@blampey2024novae] expects an `.h5ad` file with gene
expression and spatial coordinates. It uses an attentional graph neural
network to produce embeddings and spatially-aware domain assignments. The
output is an `AnnData` object: embeddings at `adata$obsm$novae_latent`,
domain assignments at `adata$obs$novae_domains_7`. Novae runs on GPUs and
Apple Metal Performance Shaders (MPS).

**Prov-GigaPath** [@xu2024gigapath] is a whole-slide foundation model for
computational pathology trained on real-world H&E data. It pairs a
DINOv2-pretrained ViT-giant tile encoder (1536-dim) with a LongNet slide
encoder (768-dim), enabling local-morphology and global-context
representations. Integration is in progress; the current obstacle is that
the required Python environment is slow to provision because of the Python
version download time, and we are still working out a robust pattern for
remote GPU servers.

**Nimbus** [@rumberger2025automated] is built on a U-Net backbone widely
used in biomedical image analysis. It accepts each marker channel of a
multiplexed image and returns a per-cell positivity score plus annotations,
making it broadly applicable across multiplexed-imaging platforms.

### Use case: KRONOS

KRONOS [@shaban2025foundation] is a foundation model purpose-built for
spatial proteomics. It was trained on more than 47 million image patches
covering 16 tissue types and 175 protein markers, and uses a Vision
Transformer based on DINOv2 modified to handle highly multiplexed images.
KRONOS extracts 384-dimensional embeddings for each patch, which can be used
for cell phenotyping, artifact detection, or outcome prediction.

KRONOS is not distributed as a Python package, so it does not yet fit the
`BiocFoundationGPU` envelope. We instead explored it directly in a use case
of interest. Starting from a multiplexed image with ~56 channels
(@fig-kronos-input), we applied KRONOS to 128 × 128 sliding windows to
obtain feature vectors. PCA of these embeddings, viewed spatially as
RGB-mapped per-window boxes, immediately surfaces a clear image artifact
(@fig-kronos-pca).

![Original DAPI channel of the multiplexed input
image.](../figures/team_foundationmodels/Figure_origDapi.png){#fig-kronos-input width=70%}

::: {#fig-kronos-pca}
*Figure placeholder.* First three principal components of the KRONOS
feature vectors, mapped to RGB and laid out in their original spatial
positions. The pattern strongly indicates an image artifact. The original
PNG (`features_pca.png`) lives in the Drive `team_foundationmodels/`
folder; needs re-download — see open issue.
:::

## End-to-end compatibility with `SpatialExperiment`

The package design takes a `SpatialExperiment` in and returns embeddings in
the same object. Because most Python tools expect `AnnData`, we extended
`anndataR` to support bidirectional conversion between `SpatialExperiment`
and `AnnData`, and built an evaluation pipeline to compare round-tripped
objects. That comparison surfaced a real mismatch in spatial-data processing
that we are working through.

Once the conversion logic stabilizes and the remaining inconsistencies are
resolved through systematic testing, the implementation will be consolidated
and submitted as a pull request to `anndataR`. The current development branch
can be installed with:

```r
remotes::install_github("drighelli/anndataR@from_as_spe")
```

### `anndataR` ↔ `SpatialExperiment` interoperability

We extended `anndataR` to support bidirectional conversion between
`SpatialExperiment` and `AnnData` by layering a spatially aware translation
on top of the existing `SingleCellExperiment` conversion framework, preserving
canonical mappings for assays, feature- and cell-level annotations,
dimensionality reductions, and graph-like pairwise structures while adding
explicit handling for spatial state. Going forward (R → Python),
`spatialCoords` are serialized to `obsm["spatial"]` and image-associated
information from `imgData` is mapped to `uns["spatial"]` using a
library-indexed nested representation compatible with Scanpy conventions;
going back (Python → R), these structures are parsed to reconstruct
`SpatialExperiment` objects with restored spatial coordinates and image
metadata.

The central technical difficulty is that `SpatialExperiment` carries
semantics that are not native to the core `AnnData` object model: spatial
coordinates are not generic embeddings, image metadata are heterogeneous
and partially technology-specific, and multi-library datasets require a
deterministic reconciliation between observation-level sample annotations
and image-level library identifiers. To address these we introduced
reserved spatial keys, conflict checks against user-defined `obsm` and
`uns` mappings, and an auxiliary round-trip metadata container in
`uns["anndataR_spatialexperiment"]` that preserves otherwise non-canonical
information such as original spatial coordinate names, selected spatial
keys, library identifier provenance, and image export mode. We further
implemented explicit import/export policies for image payloads — embedded,
reference-based, or omitted — to balance interoperability, file size, and
reversibility.

### Testing the conversion

Further validation work is needed. Preliminary evaluation suggests that
discrepancies still emerge in specific configurations, particularly those
involving technology-specific spatial metadata or image representations.
The reproducibility case study in the `renovae` repository
(<https://github.com/sales-lab/renovae>) documents one such mismatch.
Targeted analysis of these reproducibility cases will help us identify
remaining failure points in the conversion logic.
